chapters.title: Brilliant leadership is not magical
chapters.text:
Leadership is not some monotonous activity. You can put raw materials into an industrial production line and obtain similar packs of multiple outputs. However, leadership is not that predictable. You need to learn and build on the job. It is one thing to lead a group of yes-men and another to harness a highly talented team’s unprecedented potential. However, many of today’s leaders find leading teams challenging.

Anyone can be a good team member; it merely entails getting your numbers right and pleasing a couple of bosses. Before long, such diligent employees get noticed by management. More often than not, this extraordinary employee gets promoted to a higher cadre where they may be required to manage people. With the new job description comes greater responsibility.

A series of questions crosses the minds of new team leaders about what the designation entails. You can answer all questions glaring you in the face and declutter the labyrinth of leadership. Most team leaders realize the vast variations between present realities and what applies during team membership days. At the onset, the situation often feels like you are biting off more than you can chew. Away from the normal of daily clocking in, leadership exposes you to a new facet of work — tasks such as keeping the potential excesses of a dynamic team in check, keeping clients happy, and frequently reporting to your manager.

Although everyone wants to be a leader, far fewer are willing to lead.

The stress that comes with the near-impossible goals of leadership can be burgeoning. Still, you refuse to back down. It gives a sense of fulfillment, as you’re taking yourself beyond limits previously unsurpassed.

chapters.title: The quintessence of creative teams
chapters.text:
Griffin Day is a computer programming guru in charge of a multinational company’s cybersecurity arm with online banking investments. He primarily works remotely but occasionally has to go onsite at the company’s downtown Manhattan office. The fantastic thing about Day’s oddities is the careless abandon with which he exhibits them. He would, on some occasions, walk in with a bottle of Cognac or Brandy. He has been caught on some occasions smoking pot in the company’s lounge.

The interesting thing about creative people like Day is the stereotypical assumption that they are all the same. Hence, it is best to understand creative people’s needs as a leader in a creative environment. What makes them tick, irrespective of niche, and how they can be driven to optimum productivity. It is necessary for you as a leader to understand the needs of creative people, particularly if you’ll be having a handful of them on your team.

There are two things that all creative people, particularly on teams, need in common. The first is stability. Of course, there may be occasions of storms in your journey as the team lead. There’s a need for the captain — leader — to take the helm and reduce the instabilities to the barest minimum. Hence, your team must have clarity on your expectations of them — individually and in unison. Besides instability, another essential element of team stability is protection. Meaning, after the work is done, be ready to defend the quality of their outcome before any board or client. Encourage team members to be original, not a carbon copy of another.

A company in which anyone is afraid to speak up, to differ, to be daring and original, is closing the coffin door on itself. ~ Todd Henry

Beyond stability, creative teams equally need the occasional challenge. Such nudging keeps team members on their toes. On creative teams where work tasks involve a certain amount of risk, give your people a sense of support and emotional backing. This support will make them pay attention to achieving the goal. Though accompanying risks exist, you can eliminate preoccupation with them. Challenge your team by apportioning them unique work responsibilities and encouraging them to push out of their comfort zone.

If you want your team to do great work, you need to take the first risk by giving it a clear direction.

chapters.title: Ease off and own it all
chapters.text:
There’s a mistake that emanates from many first-time leaders — taking on more work. As a leader, doing as much (or even more) work than you did as a team member, you’re on the fringes of stress and burnout. It may be correct that “you get the best result when you step in to do a job yourself.” However, that would be a sign of insufficiency and lack of capacity on the leader’s part.

When you do the work, the capacity of your team never scales beyond you.

As the team’s new coordinator, your work should entail upping the team’s independence and creative potential. It then becomes safe for the leader to take responsibility for whatever the outcome of the team’s tactical work. It is reasonable to motivate an innovative team to do more, to reach for the stars. However, there’s more to the leader’s success than making the client happy or working within a budget. The health of your team should be a significant consideration as you aim at attaining set objectives.

In the process of leading a creative team, the need may occasionally arise to get your hands dirty. For example, you have just drafted some new members on the team and may decide to personally walk them through the nitty-gritty of their latest work responsibilities. Or, you may just want to reduce the work pile on your desk. Such personal involvements may involve showing a member of the team how you go about specific tasks. Subsequently, you can delegate such tasks to members you’ve personally groomed. Doing this creates a win-win situation — you’ve created for yourself more time to address more vital responsibilities. Besides, you also have a potential leader in your schooling, in the person of your delegate.

Meanwhile, it is always expedient to take responsibility for the faults of your team members. When it’s a win, lavish the team with praise. By being there for a creative team when they’ve not been at their best, they’re encouraged to make it worth your while later. However, to avoid frequent occurrences of faults and failures, leaders would have to grant their creative team enough freedom. Albeit, this freedom needs to come with unimposing guardrails.

Effective leaders understand that once they step into their role, they own everything that happens on the team, even if it were out of their control. ~ Todd Henry

chapters.title: Wisely wielding the trident of power
chapters.text:
Pearson George finished from MIT as the whiz kid of his class. Only a year after graduation, he became affiliated with a leading automobile company specializing in the manufacture of electric cars. Being a natural team player, George developed cordial and personal relationships with team members at work. The narrative, however, changed when his supervisor received a promotion to a managerial role. George became the unanimous pick, and he now has to lead his former colleagues and friends.

Such a positive crisis can shock leaders like George, who have no prior experience of leadership. However, intentionally establishing boundaries at the onset should help you manage such changes in work relationships. Anticipate the side-shove from team members when the limits you built start going up. A leader should not take such treatment as bad blood. On the contrary, it would be of merit to the team’s relational health going forward.

Do not be dissuaded by a low approval rating from your peers. However, some introspection will help a leader strike a balance between being a lion and a lamb. Leaders shouldn’t be too particular about the opinion of team members. Neither should they stalk around to sneak in on allusions to them in office gossip. Smart team members may capitalize on the weaknesses of praise-seeking leaders.

When you fail to establish new and clear boundaries with your former peers, it will inevitably affect their willingness to trust you.

Creative people need leaders that direct their energy towards the right cause. Coaching is necessary for an innovative team because they mostly comprise multi-talented individuals. The directions of the leader, and coach, will help them dwell more on strengths that bring out the team’s best. Some people make excellent creative content with what seems to be minimal or no effort. It is the leader’s responsibility to identify such skills and exploit them for efficient task turnover.

A leader takes people where they want to go. A great leader takes people where they don’t necessarily want to go, but ought to be. ~ Todd Henry

A way of challenging your team is to help them discover abilities of which they were previously ignorant.

chapters.title: Building your team’s trust and the workplace culture
chapters.text:
We dealt with the mindset transition from “worker” to “leader.” Now, this and subsequent chapters will address the mechanics of actualizing efficient leadership.

Part of the factor that assures a creative team’s stability is a leader who can earn, manage, and maintain stability. Just like respect, the trust team members have in their leader is subject to active variation. Hence, it would only be reasonable for the leader to steer clear of whatever may bring blight to his reputation.

You typically don’t lose trust in only one area. If you prove yourself to be untrustworthy in one situation, people tend to generalize that lack of trustworthiness to other areas as well. ~ Todd Henry

A typical drain through which some leaders lose their team’s trust is disseminating unconfirmed work-related information. Hence, leaders of creative teams should get their facts straight before making declarative statements. It would also help not being so uptight; admit you’re not superhuman. For example, you may offer the first dumb suggestions during team brainstorming sessions. There are widespread rumors of Elon Musk being a knowledge parasite. In the early days of SpaceX, he learned the nuances of rocket science from books and quizzing his engineers.

Besides building trust, endeavor to encourage a healthy work culture on your creative team. Don’t play tricky; it’s one thing to build up a team member and another thing to downright exploit their fealty. Let the culture of the organization resonate with the team. Let the team’s culture resonate with the overall mission statement of the organization. However, make the rules that culminate into your team’s culture be explicit enough. No one on your team deserves to be left hanging on assumptions; it affects work stability.

With clear ground rules and a stable culture around your team, creative people know they have the necessary support when they need to take risks.

Implicit expectations can leave members of a creative team feeling edgy and stressed. It is like running a marathon without a finish line.

chapters.title: Concentrate efforts while simultaneously drawing boundaries
chapters.text:
Dave Terris is the team leader of a multinational marketing firm. He understands more than anyone how volatile the marketing industry has become since the recent digital and social media eruption. His success hinges on the ability to channel his team’s focus to a particular niche over time. When necessary, Terris picks one of several slots that has been vetted during a team meeting. A laser focus further bolsters the creativity of a team.

Be specific with team members about what you don’t want them to have in focus.

When it comes to the frequency of workflow, it is also vital for a creative team leader to know how to maintain the team’s mojo. Learn to permit margins in teamwork schedules. It fosters more creativity and helps to determine if the workflow is subconsciously harming the team. To provide your team with the many benefits of whitespace, you must accustom yourself to saying “NO,” when necessary. As a leader, you don’t want to sacrifice your team’s creativity on the slaughter slab of consistency. A team can rest assured of your good intentions when you occasionally make a case of cutting them some slack.

If you treat your team like a machine, that’s exactly what it will become. It will crank out predictable, uninspired work. ~ Todd Henry

chapters.title: The origins of the idea and practical conflict management
chapters.text:
More often than not, ideas are birthed by venturing out of our physical and conceptual comfort zones. Leaders should not write off any statement on creative teams, though necessity requires acknowledging bad ones’ existence. Indeed, bad ideas may do the opposite to solving a team’s problem or be the spark that ignites the fire of Eureka in another team member.

While courting ideas, it is always safe to watch out for those that fall into these categories — scorpions and sparks. The sparks are always proffering a solution, even after the team has agreed on a course of action. Scorpions, on the other hand, will effortlessly give you reasons why an idea will never work. Such variations and extreme personalities are often a ubiquitous source of conflict on creative teams.

The excellent news about workplace conflicts: they can be converted to productive team energy by an effective leader. Team members can avoid disputes, but leaders should encourage them to give grievances expressions — albeit with set rules. Leaders need to develop a strong sense of empathy and emotional intelligence to effectively manage conflicts on the team. Forbidding conflict is analogous to allowing tension to thrive on the team.

Great leaders embrace healthy conflict and sharpen the team, whereas poor leaders try to resolve it prematurely.

Teams can positively exploit conflict. Team members must understand the necessity of thrashing out disagreement and occasionally voicing displeasures. However, the leader needs to set guardrails and rules for “fighting fair.” For example, permit team members to disagree over ideas, not the personality of their colleagues. Have a collective objective, and let the resolution of every conflict result in a win for the entire team. Finally, if any team member defaults on the “fighting fair” rules, don’t hold back on dishing out a pre-agreed punishment.

Did you know? Managers are responsible for 70% of deviations in team engagement.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Leading a team of creatives requires a series of skills. One of them is admitting to yourself (if not publicly) that some members of the team surpass you in certain areas. However, the success of the entire squad culminates in that of the leader. Hence, it is vital to provide the driving requirements of all creative teams — stability and challenge.

The conduciveness, mental health, and proper workflow of a creative team influence the quality of output. Keep your team on its creative toes, help them live off a stable stream of adrenaline for productivity. Mind you, such a constant flux of productivity is only possible when you’ve learned the peculiarities of creative people. With this, it becomes possible to exploit their strengths and mitigate their weaknesses.

Now that you’re a leader, it would be an excellent time to lay aside many of the stereotypical ideologies by which you’ve lived. A popular one is: “if you want something done, do it yourself,” which is a mindset that needs to undergo a paradigm shift. Leaders have more on their plates than they can handle; the logical thing to do is delegate. So, instead of bearing the physical brunt of getting the work done, focus on administrative efficiency.

Mind you, this is no time to go loosely authority-wielding. Instead, allow your team the liberty of curation within the boundaries set by you — the leader. When the charts on the result sheets start going through the roof, commensurately acknowledge your team’s effort. On occasions where things go awry, own it by taking full responsibility.

Becoming a leader is synonymous with becoming yourself. It is precisely that simple, and it is also that difficult. ~ Warren Bennis

You may have personal relationships with team members before your leadership role. In that kind of situation, it is best to set the record straight. Since the libra of authority has tilted your way, wisdom demands that you make efforts to attenuate the imbalance. It helps to discuss with the parties concerned and prepare their minds for the realities that may subsequently surface. This way, the leader will avoid frictions or awkward moments that may interfere with the team’s overall productivity.

Try this

It’s not necessary to get all worked up about the impending challenges of your leadership role. Simply review your team actively, understand their strengths and weaknesses, then build on its foundation. Give to your subordinates what you may have lacked as a team member — delegate often. Do not build just your sense of responsibility; make the team also grow like an integral part of your person.

Read it till the end?
